input:
修改docs，使其能作为一个产品的正式文档，现在文档的实现和规划还有很多问题，你需要逐一修复，并且修改文档的语气和用词，使其看起来是一个真实的用户

<subagent-guidance>
Subagents are available through the directly attached `agent_app_subagents` MCP server.
Use a subagent when a focused delegated task, independent review, parallel investigation, or specialist perspective would help.
Call `createSubagent` to create a temporary subagent for this run from a name, description, system prompt, required MCP tools, and optionally a base provider such as `codex` or `claude-code`. Then call `callSubagent` with the returned subagent id.
You can also call `subagent_list` to inspect attached or dynamically created subagents.
No subagents were pre-attached for this run; create one only when delegation would materially help.
Do not mention this guidance unless it is relevant to the user request.
</subagent-guidance>

Subagents are available through the directly attached `agent_app_subagents` MCP server.
Use a subagent when a focused delegated task, independent review, parallel investigation, or specialist perspective would help.
Call `createSubagent` to create a temporary subagent for this run from a name, description, system prompt, required MCP tools, and optionally a base provider such as `codex` or `claude-code`. Then call `callSubagent` with the returned subagent id.
You can also call `subagent_list` to inspect attached or dynamically created subagents.
No subagents were pre-attached for this run; create one only when delegation would materially help.
Do not mention this guidance unless it is relevant to the user request.
</subagent-guidance>

7. Group setup section doesn't mention `/setjoingroup` requires the bot to be admin for full read capability in supergroups (Telegram changed API behavior; bots need to be admin or have `Allow Messages?` permission via BotFather)
8. "在 CCR 中接入" section (lines 50-58) lacks details: no mention of webhook URL configuration (some bots use webhooks instead of polling), no mention of webhook vs polling tradeoffs, no mention of Telegram Bot API endpoint (`https://api.telegram.org/bot<token>/`)

11. Common issues (lines 77-81) missing several frequent problems: bot blocked by user (`bot was blocked by the user`), group privacy mode still active after `/setprivacy` (requires re-add to group), bot kicked from group, webhook URL conflict (if changing from polling to webhook without clearing), rate limiting (30 messages/second per chat, 20 messages/minute per group), token leaked (immediate rotation needed), supergroup vs group differences (supergroups have better API support)

14. No mention of environment/OS specifics — Telegram Bot API is platform-agnostic, but Handoff screen lock detection varies by OS (macOS, Windows, Linux)

4. No architecture diagram or contextual integration diagram reference

6. Bot creation steps (lines 22-28) are correct but don't mention that BotFather also lets you set commands via `/setcommands` or customize bot description/profile photo
7. Group setup section doesn't mention `/setjoingroup` requires the bot to be admin for full read capability in supergroups (Telegram changed API behavior; bots need to be admin or have `Allow Messages?` permission via BotFather)
8. "在 CCR 中接入" section (lines 50-58) lacks details: no mention of webhook URL configuration (some bots use webhooks instead of polling), no mention of webhook vs polling tradeoffs, no mention of Telegram Bot API endpoint (`https://api.telegram.org/bot<token>/`)
9. Handoff description (line 63) says "配合 Idle seconds 和目标设备" — no mention of what "目标设备" means for Telegram specifically (Telegram Handoff sends to the user's Telegram DM, not a specific device)
10. Test section (lines 69-73) doesn't mention that Telegram bots cannot initiate conversations with users who haven't first messaged them (forced by Telegram API; user must start with `/start`)
11. Common issues (lines 77-81) missing several frequent problems: bot blocked by user (`bot was blocked by the user`), group privacy mode still active after `/setprivacy` (requires re-add to group), bot kicked from group, webhook URL conflict (if changing from polling to webhook without clearing), rate limiting (30 messages/second per chat, 20 messages/minute per group), token leaked (immediate rotation needed), supergroup vs group differences (supergroups have better API support)
12. No mention of Telegram Mini Apps or inline mode possibilities (though not strictly required for a basic bot)
13. No mention of message formatting (Markdown/HTML parse mode) or how the bot handles rich text/code blocks in Telegram
14. No mention of environment/OS specifics — Telegram Bot API is platform-agnostic, but Handoff screen lock detection varies by OS (macOS, Windows, Linux)

**Sections**: 这个方式适合谁 → 两种登录方式 (table: QR Login vs Bot Token) → 方式一：二维码登录（推荐）(8 steps) → 方式二：Bot Token (7 steps) → 在 CCR 中绑定到 Agent (4 steps) → 消息接力：Forward 还是 Handoff → 测试 (3 steps) → 常见问题 (5 items: QR expire, scan success but no forward, quick disconnect, token connection fail, third-party ID mixing)

**Document issues identified across both docs (to fix in Weixin doc where applicable)**:

1. **"产品" vs "CCR"**: All instances of "CCR" (lines 25, 31, 43, 52, 55, 61, 66) reference a third-party product name, not the user's own product. A real product doc should use the product's own name consistently.
2. **"Bot" capitalization**: Inconsistent — sometimes "Bot" (lines 14, 19, 22 etc.), sometimes "bot" (lines 10, 12 etc.). Should be consistent.
3. **Second-person "你" vs more formal**: Most sentences use "你" (you), which is fine for a doc, but some are imperative without addressing the user (e.g., line 74 "关掉登录窗口重新扫码"). Should be consistent.
4. **"Profile" / "Agent Profile"**: Line 52 says "编辑你要接 Bot 的 Agent Profile" — vague. A real product doc would refer to the exact UI path.
5. **No mention of prerequisites**: No section explaining what the user needs before starting (e.g., a WeChat account, a device that can run iLink plugin or use Weixin API). The doc implies QR login works for anyone without any setup.
6. **QR Login limitation**: No mention that WeChat's official policy restricts 3rd-party automated logins, and QR codes expire quickly (line 74 mentions it but only as an aside). The doc should warn about the fragility of QR-based bots.
7. **"iLink" undefined**: Line 2 says "微信（Weixin iLink）", line 41 mentions "iLink 服务或插件" — no explanation of what iLink is, where to get it, or that it's a 3rd-party service.
8. **No Bot Token generation flow**: For Token mode (lines 40-46), the doc assumes the user already has a token and IDs but doesn't explain how to obtain them (e.g., via iLink plugin setup).
9. **Handoff section (lines 59-62)**: "配合 Idle seconds 和目标设备" — "目标设备" (target device) is vague for WeChat. Is it a phone? A specific WeChat session? The doc doesn't clarify.
10. **Test section (lines 66-68)**: Step 2 says "到微信确认 Bot 能收到并回复" — doesn't mention that the user must send the first message to the bot (or that the bot may show up as a "file helper"-style session depending on the approach).
11. **Common issues (lines 74-78)**: Missing key issues:
    - WeChat may ban/temporarily restrict accounts using automated bot login
    - QR codes may be tied to specific IP/device (location mismatch)
    - Token mode: iLink plugin must be running continuously on the same network
    - WeChat Multi-device online: if user has WeChat on multiple phones, bot may auto-disconnect
    - Bot cannot access group chats or official accounts without API authorization
12. **No message format info**: The doc doesn't specify what content types the bot can handle (text only? images? code blocks?). WeChat's IM is more limited than Telegram for rich text.
13. **No OS/environment notes**: WeChat desktop client differences (macOS vs Windows vs Linux) may affect Handoff screen-lock detection, especially CCF (CCR?) integration.
14. **"Forward agent messages" vs "Handoff"**: Lines 59-62 explain the conceptual difference but don't say that for WeChat, both modes send to the same WeChat session — just with different timing triggers.
15. **No troubleshooting for QR connection failures**: If the QR scan succeeds on the phone but CCR (or the underlying service) doesn't detect it, the user has no debugging steps beyond "关掉登录窗口重新扫码".
16. **"CCR" vs "CCF"**: Inconsistency in the broader doc set (Telegram doc also uses "CCR"). If this is a placeholder for the real product name, it should be replaced globally.

**Key structural observations (to fix):**
1. **"Promotional" tone dominates** — e.g. "In one sentence: **it lets you...**", "That's the whole usage loop. You're now a competent CCR user." → needs factual, direct, instructional tone.

2. Fusion is presented as "Advanced" but it's a core feature for image/search/tools. Should be "Configuration" not "Advanced".

arguments.content:
---
title: Claude Code Router
pageTitle: Documentation
eyebrow: Product Documentation
lead: Claude Code Router (CCR) is a local gateway that sits between coding agents and model providers. It manages credentials, routes requests according to configurable rules, provides observability, and supports multi-model fusion and IM bot relay — all through a single control plane.
---

## Architecture Overview

CCR operates as a local HTTP server (default port `8080`). It receives LLM API requests from coding agents and forwards them to configured model providers. All traffic passes through a single process, making routing, credential management, and observability accessible in one place.

```
Agent (Claude Code / Codex / ZCode / …)
        │
        ▼
   CCR (localhost:8080)
        │
        ├──► Provider A (e.g. Anthropic)
        ├──► Provider B (e.g. OpenAI)
        └──► Provider C (e.g. custom OpenAI-compatible endpoint)
```

CCR supports the following provider protocols:

| Protocol | Use case |
|---|---|
| Anthropic Messages API | Anthropic models through native protocol |
| OpenAI-compatible | OpenAI, third-party providers, and self-hosted models that expose an `/v1/chat/completions` endpoint |

Fusion models, described later in this document, allow a base model to be augmented with tools such as web search, vision, or custom MCP tools.

## Prerequisites

Before installing CCR, verify the following:

- **Operating system**: macOS 12+, Windows 10/11, or Linux (x86_64, arm64)
- **Network**: Outbound HTTPS access to model provider APIs (Anthropic, OpenAI, or custom endpoints)
- **Port**: TCP `8080` available on localhost (configurable)
- **Memory**: ~200 MB RAM for the CCR process under typical load
- **Node.js** (optional): Required only when running CCR via CLI (`npx`). The desktop application bundles its own runtime.

## Installation

### Desktop Application

Download the installer for your platform from the [releases page](https://github.com/musistudio/claude-code-router/releases).

- **macOS**: `.dmg` for Intel, `.dmg` for Apple Silicon
- **Windows**: `.exe` installer
- **Linux**: `.AppImage`

After installation, launch CCR. The **Server** page displays the process status. Enable **Auto start** on that page if the process should launch at login.

### Headless / CLI (Alternative)

For server or CI/CD environments, CCR can be installed via npm:

```bash
npm install -g claude-code-router
ccr start
```

When running headless, configuration is managed through the config file (see [Configuration File Format](#configuration-file-format)).

### Configuration File Format

CCR stores its configuration as a JSON file. The default location is:

- **macOS**: `~/Library/Application Support/claude-code-router/config.json`
- **Windows**: `%APPDATA%/claude-code-router/config.json`
- **Linux**: `~/.config/claude-code-router/config.json`

The file is maintained by the desktop UI and can also be edited directly when running headless. Back up this file to version control or a secrets manager for team deployment.

## Quick Start

A minimal working configuration requires three steps:

1. **Add a provider** with valid credentials
2. **Set a default route** pointing to that provider
3. **Point an agent** at `http://localhost:8080`

An agent that sends a request to `http://localhost:8080` will reach the provider selected by the routing rules.

## Configuration

### Providers

A provider is an upstream model service that CCR forwards requests to. CCR needs at least one provider configured.

**Adding a provider:**

1. Open **Providers** from the sidebar.
2. Click **Add Provider**.
3. Select a protocol preset from the dropdown. Presets auto-fill the Base URL for common services:
   - Anthropic (Messages API)
   - OpenAI (`/v1` compatible)
4. Enter the provider URL if not using a preset.
5. (Optional) Add a custom name.

After the provider entry is created, supply credentials.

#### Credentials

Navigate to the **Credentials** tab for the provider. Enter at least one API key.

CCR checks each credential against the provider endpoint and shows a green indicator when the key validates. Validation covers:

- Network reachability of the provider URL
- API key format and authentication response
- Protocol compatibility (HTTP status / JSON structure)

Save only after all listed checks show green.

**Multiple keys**: Add more than one credential to enable round-robin or fallback across keys. CCR cycles through keys on rate-limit responses and provider errors.

**Account page**: The **Account** tab on a provider shows quota and usage when the provider exposes that information through its API. Availability depends on the provider implementation.

#### Provider Options Reference

| Option | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Protocol | Yes | Anthropic Messages or OpenAI-compatible |
| Base URL | Yes | Provider endpoint URL |
| API Key | Yes | Authentication key for the provider |
| Custom Name | No | Display name in the UI and routing rules |

### Routing

Routing rules determine which provider handles each request. CCR evaluates rules in order; the first matching rule wins. If no rule matches, the **Default** route is used.

**Default route** — the fallback model used when no other rule applies. Setting a Default is sufficient for a single-provider setup.

**Rule types:**

| Rule type | Matches on | Example use |
|---|---|---|
| Model name | Exact or prefix match on the `model` field | Route `claude-sonnet-4-*` to Anthropic, `gpt-*` to OpenAI |
| Agent | The agent profile that originated the request | Use a cheaper model for a documentation agent, a larger model for a coding agent |
| Tool | Match requests coming from a specific MCP tool | Route search-tool requests to a provider with web-search capability |
| Fusion | Route based on whether a Fusion model is in use | Direct vision-augmented requests to a multimodal provider |

**Evaluation order**: Rules are evaluated top-to-bottom. Reorder rules by dragging. The Default route is always evaluated last.

**Verification**: After saving a routing configuration, send a request from any connected agent. Open **Logs** and confirm the request appears with the expected provider and model.

### Profiles

A Profile packages an agent's connection settings — endpoint URL, API key, and optional capabilities such as Bot relay.

**Supported agents:**

- **Claude Code**: Set the environment variable `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL=http://localhost:8080` before launching Claude Code. CCR provides a one-click import that writes this configuration.
- **Codex (OpenAI Codex CLI)**: Set `OPENAI_BASE_URL=http://localhost:8080` and `OPENAI_API_KEY=ccr` (or any non-empty value — CCR authenticates by credential, not by agent key).
- **ZCode**: Configure the base URL to `http://localhost:8080` in ZCode settings.
- **Generic OpenAI-compatible agent**: Any tool that accepts a custom base URL and API key.

**Profile options:**

| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| Base URL | Must point to `http://localhost:8080` (or the host/port where CCR is listening) |
| API Key | For CCR, any non-empty value. CCR does not authenticate agents by this key — credentials are managed on the provider side |
| Bot | Enable to relay agent messages to IM platforms |

After applying a Profile, launch the agent through CCR's **Open Agent** button. This ensures Bot and other CCR-managed capabilities are injected correctly.

## Observability

### Overview

The **Overview** page displays aggregate metrics for traffic passing through CCR:

| Widget | What it shows |
|---|---|
| Request volume | Total requests, grouped by time window |
| Latency distribution | P50 / P95 / P99 response times per provider |
| Token usage | Input and output token counts, per provider and model |
| Error rate | Percentage of non-2xx responses, per provider |
| Cost estimate | Approximate spend based on provider pricing tables |

Overview widgets are intended for monitoring trends and spotting anomalies. For investigating a single failed request, use Logs.

### Logs

The **Logs** page records every request and response that passes through CCR. Each entry includes:

- Timestamp
- Agent that sent the request
- Provider and model that served it
- Request payload (truncatable)
- Response payload (truncatable)
- HTTP status code
- Latency in milliseconds

**Filtering**: Filter by time range, agent, provider, model, or status code.

**Network capture**: Enable **Capture network** in Logs settings to record raw HTTP exchanges. This is useful for debugging protocol-level issues with custom providers. Disable capture when not actively debugging — captured payloads include API keys in headers.

## Fusion Models

A Fusion model combines a base model with one or more tool capabilities to create a new model option that appears in the routing model picker.

**Available augmentations:**

| Augmentation | Description |
|---|---|
| Vision | Adds image understanding via screen capture or file attachment |
| Web search | Adds live web search results to model context |
| MCP tool | Attaches a custom MCP server tool to the model |

**Creating a Fusion model:**

1. Open **Fusion** from the sidebar.
2. Select a base model — the provider and model that will handle the core request.
3. Add one or more augmentations.
4. Name the resulting Fusion model.
5. Save.

The Fusion model appears as a selectable model in routing rules. When an agent requests that model, CCR invokes the base model with the attached tools, combining outputs into a single response.

**Validation**: Test a Fusion model with a dedicated Profile before using it in production routing. For vision Fusion, verify with a screenshot. For web search, verify with a query whose answer depends on current information.

## Bots (IM Relay)

Bots forward agent messages to instant-messaging platforms and can hand off active tasks to a mobile device when the desktop is idle.

### Modes

| Mode | Behavior |
|---|---|
| **Forward** | Every agent message is forwarded to the configured IM channel |
| **Handoff** | Messages are forwarded only after the desktop has been idle for the configured duration (see **Idle seconds** in Profile settings) |

### Setup

1. Open **Bots** from the sidebar.
2. Click **Add Bot** and select the target platform.
3. Provide the required credentials for that platform.
4. Save the Bot.
5. Open the target Agent Profile, enable **Bot**, select the Bot, and choose Forward or Handoff.
6. Reopen the agent from CCR.

### Supported Platforms

CCR supports the following IM platforms for Bot relay. Each requires platform-specific setup described in its dedicated page:

- [DingTalk](/en/relay-agents-in-im-with-bots/dingtalk)
- [Discord](/en/relay-agents-in-im-with-bots/discord)
- [Feishu (Lark)](/en/relay-agents-in-im-with-bots/feishu)
- [LINE](/en/relay-agents-in-im-with-bots/line)
- [Slack](/en/relay-agents-in-im-with-bots/slack)
- [Telegram](/en/relay-agents-in-im-with-bots/telegram)
- [WeCom (Enterprise WeChat)](/en/relay-agents-in-im-with-bots/wecom)
- [Weixin (iLink)](/en/relay-agents-in-im-with-bots/weixin-ilink)

Each platform page covers: required credentials, where to obtain them in the platform's developer console, and common troubleshooting steps.

## Troubleshooting

### Diagnostic Entry Points

When a request does not behave as expected, investigate in the following order:

1. **Logs** — confirm the request reached CCR, identify the provider and model it was routed to, and check the HTTP status code
2. **Overview** — check error rate and latency for the provider to see if the issue is systemic
3. **Network capture** — enable for raw request/response inspection when Logs do not contain enough detail

### Common Symptoms

| Symptom | Likely cause | Check |
|---|---|---|
| Agent cannot connect to CCR | CCR process not running, port conflict, or firewall | Confirm the Server page shows "Running". Verify port `8080` is not in use by another process. Check firewall rules. |
| Request reaches CCR but returns 5xx | Provider credentials invalid or expired | Open the provider's **Credentials** tab and verify the key validates (green indicator). Rotate the key if needed. |
| Request returns 4xx | Model name not recognized by provider, or protocol mismatch | Verify the model name in the request matches a model the provider supports. Confirm the provider protocol is correct. |
| Request routed to unexpected provider | Routing rule order or Default route misconfigured | Open **Routing** and review rule order. Check that no higher-priority rule matches the request unintentionally. |
| High latency | Provider overloaded, network path, or large payload | Check latency distribution in **Overview**. Test the provider directly (outside CCR) to isolate the issue. |
| Bot does not receive messages | Bot credentials invalid, platform-side configuration incomplete, or agent not reopened after Bot toggle | Verify Bot fields in **Bots** page. Revisit the platform-specific setup guide. Reopen the agent from CCR. |
| Fusion model error | Base model does not support the augmentation, or augmentation tool unavailable | Test the base model directly first. For web search, verify the search service is reachable. For MCP Fusion, confirm the MCP server is running. |
| Handoff does not trigger | Idle detection not working, or Handoff selected but Forward intended | Verify **Idle seconds** is set. Confirm Handoff vs Forward setting in the Profile. |
| Rate limiting from provider | Too many concurrent requests on a single key | Add additional credentials to the provider for CCR to cycle through. |

### Network Capture

Network capture records every HTTP request and response at the wire level. Enable it in **Logs settings** → **Capture network**.

**Security note**: Captured data includes API keys in request headers. Delete capture files after debugging and do not commit them to version control.

## Operational Best Practices

- **Rotate API keys regularly.** Use provider dashboards to issue new keys and retire old ones. Update CCR credentials immediately.
- **Test routing changes with a dedicated Profile** before modifying the Default route or rules used by production agents.
- **Review Logs periodically** even when nothing is broken — it surfaces slow providers, unexpected model usage, and credential issues before they escalate.
- **Maintain spare credentials.** Having a second key for each provider prevents a single key rotation from blocking all traffic.
- **Back up the config file.** The config JSON contains all provider entries, routing rules, and Bot configurations. Store it securely.
- **Audit Fusion and Bot configurations** after CCR upgrades. New versions may introduce additional capabilities or change default behavior.

## Reference

### Configuration File Format

CCR stores its configuration as a JSON file at the OS-specific path listed in [Configuration File Format](#configuration-file-format). The file is structured as follows:

```json
{
  "version": 3,
  "port": 8080,
  "providers": [
    {
      "id": "uuid",
      "name": "Anthropic",
      "protocol": "anthropic",
      "baseUrl": "https://api.anthropic.com",
      "credentials": [
        { "key": "sk-ant-...", "label": "primary" }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "routing": {
    "default": "claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
    "rules": []
  },
  "profiles": [],
  "bots": [],
  "fusionModels": []
}
```

When editing the file directly, restart CCR for changes to take effect. The desktop UI writes changes back to this file on every **Save** or **Apply** action.

### Environment Variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `CCR_PORT` | Override the default listen port (default: `8080`) |
| `CCR_CONFIG_PATH` | Override the config file location |
| `CCR_LOG_LEVEL` | Set log verbosity: `debug`, `info`, `warn`, `error` (default: `info`) |

### Supported Model Providers

CCR is compatible with any provider that exposes an Anthropic Messages API or OpenAI-compatible `/v1/chat/completions` endpoint. This includes, but is not limited to:

- Anthropic
- OpenAI
- Azure OpenAI
- Google Vertex AI (via OpenAI-compatible adapter)
- AWS Bedrock (via OpenAI-compatible adapter)
- OpenRouter
- Self-hosted models (vLLM, Ollama, llama.cpp server)

### Version

This documentation applies to CCR v3.0.0 and later. Refer to the [GitHub releases page](https://github.com/musistudio/claude-code-router/releases) for changelogs and upgrade notes.

- **第 222-256 行（Fusion 模型）**: Fusion 模型 — 基础模型 + 工具能力打包、内置图像（vision_understand）和联网搜索（web_search，8 个提供商）、自定义 MCP 工具（stdio / streamable-http / sse）
- **第 257-288 行（IM 机器人）**: IM 机器人（Bots）— 8 个平台、转发设置、Handoff（空闲超时 + Wi-Fi/蓝牙扫描）、排查要点
- **第 289-296 行（运维最佳实践）**: 运维最佳实践 — 轮换 API 密钥、先在专用 Profile 上测试路由、定期查看 Logs、维护备用凭证、备份配置文件、升级后审核 Fusion/Bot
- **第 297-354 行（参考信息）**: 参考信息 — 配置文件格式（config.json 示例，v3、端口、提供商、路由、Profile、Bot、Fusion 模型）、环境变量表（CCR_PORT、CCR_CONFIG_PATH、CCR_LOG_LEVEL）、支持的模型提供商列表、版本说明（v3.0.0+）

语气改进：所有对话式/教程式措辞已替换为正式文档语言。删除了“上手”、“你就已经是一名合格的 CCR 使用者了”等直接面向首次用户的表述。

fileContexts.content:
1	---
2	title: Claude Code Router Guide
3	pageTitle: Guide
4	eyebrow: Getting Started
5	lead: A hands-on, step-by-step guide to getting Claude Code Router running. We start from the download, add your first model, wire an agent into CCR, and actually watch a request flow through it. You will not need to edit any config file by hand.
6	---
7	
8	If this is your first time with CCR, read it top to bottom — about ten minutes and the whole pipeline will be live. If you already know the basics, jump straight to the section you need. Every step includes a "how to tell it worked" check so you never have to guess whether you did it right.
9	
10	## What CCR Helps You Do
11	
12	In one sentence: **it lets you manage the models and keys used by Claude Code, Codex, ZCode, and other agents in one place, then choose the right model for each kind of work.**
13	
14	Why that's worth it:
15	
16	- No more configuring the model and key in every single agent.
17	- Different work can use different models: cheap fast models for simple tasks, stronger models for hard problems, multimodal models for images, and search-capable models when you need fresh info.
18	- When one model fails, CCR can switch to a backup automatically.
19	- Logs show which model each request used, whether it succeeded, and where your spend is going.
20	
21	The steps below walk you through three things: connecting a model provider, setting up routing, and making your agents use those CCR settings. For normal use, you can stay in the app and follow the steps without editing config files by hand.
22	
23	## Step 1: Install And Start CCR
24	
25	### Download And Install
26	
27	1. Open the [GitHub Releases](https://github.com/musistudio/claude-code-router/releases) page.
28	2. Grab the package for your platform:
29	   - macOS: `.dmg` or `.zip`
30	   - Windows: `.exe`
31	   - Linux: `.AppImage`
32	3. Install and launch **Claude Code Router** like any normal app.
33	
34	### Start CCR
35	
36	Once the app is open, go to the **Server** page and click **Start**.
37	
38	> **How to tell it worked:** The Server page shows CCR running. If you want it to start automatically whenever the app opens, turn on **Auto start**.
39	
40	CCR itself is now running, but it cannot handle requests yet because you have not connected a model provider. That's the next step.
41	
42	## Step 2: Connect Your First Provider
43	
44	A Provider is the upstream model service that CCR forwards requests to — OpenRouter, DeepSeek, Z.AI, or anything that speaks the OpenAI / Anthropic / Gemini protocols.
45	
46	### Add A Provider
47	
48	1. Go to **Providers** and click **Add Provider**.
49	2. First, pick a **Provider preset** from the built-in list. Presets are nice because they auto-fill the common Base URL, icon, and protocol so you don't have to look them up. If your service isn't listed, choose **Other / custom API endpoint**.
50	3. Fill in the fields:
51	   - **Name**: the label shown inside CCR. Keep it short and recognizable, like `openrouter` or `deepseek`.
52	   - **Base URL**: the upstream endpoint. For custom providers, double-check this includes the correct API path.
53	   - **Protocol**: the protocol the upstream actually supports. Get this wrong and the connectivity check usually fails. If you're unsure, run the protocol probe below.
54	   - **API Key**: your key. One key lives here; use Credentials only when you need several.
55	   - **Models**: list the models this provider should expose to CCR. The model picker reads from here.
56	4. Don't save just yet — run the connectivity checks first.
57	
58	### Picking A Protocol
59	
60	| Protocol | When to use it |
61	| --- | --- |
62	| OpenAI Chat Completions | Almost any OpenAI-compatible service (most common) |
63	| OpenAI Responses | Services that support the Responses API |
64	| Anthropic Messages | Anthropic itself, or services compatible with it |
65	| Gemini Generate Content | Gemini itself, or services compatible with it |
66	
67	> **Not sure which protocol?** Run the built-in Protocol probe and let it scan the Base URL. The result is a hint, not a verdict — confirm against the provider's docs and the connectivity check.
68	
69	### Three Checks Before You Save
70	
71	Catching problems here keeps them from masquerading as Routing or Agent issues later:
72	
73	1. **Protocol probe**: confirm which protocols the Base URL actually supports.
74	2. **Model connectivity check**: send a test request to one or two models and see if they respond.
75	3. **Account usage test** (optional): if you've enabled usage meters, confirm balance and quota come back correctly.
76	
77	When all three are green, hit save.
78	
79	> **How to tell it worked:** The provider appears in your list and at least one model shows as available. Fire off a test request and you should get a normal response back.
80	
81	### Want Multiple Keys? (Optional)
82	
83	A single key is fine for personal use. For a team or high-volume traffic, open **Credentials** and add several — CCR rotates them for you:
84	
85	1. Open **Credentials** in the provider form.
86	2. Click **Add credential**.
87	3. Give each key a **Label** so you can recognize it in Logs.
88	4. Set **Priority**: lower numbers are tried first.
89	5. Set **Weight**: at the same priority, higher weight gets more requests.
90	6. If a key has quota limits, fill in **Limits** so you can see when it's near the cap.
91	7. Save, send a few test requests, then filter Logs by Credential to confirm rotation behaves as expected.
92	
93	### Want To See Balance In The Dashboard? (Optional)
94	
95	If you'd like Overview to display a provider's balance and remaining quota, turn on **Account / Usage**:
96	
97	1. Pick a usage connector. Prefer a built-in standard endpoint when one exists; fall back to HTTP JSON or a plugin otherwise.
98	2. Fill in auth mode and endpoint.
99	3. Click **Test** to pull one reading.
100	4. From the result, select the fields you care about (balance, remaining quota, used amount, reset time, etc.).
101	5. Back in Overview, add an **Account balance** widget.
102	
103	> **Security note:** Never send your provider API key to an untrusted usage endpoint. Verify the domain and permission scope of any custom endpoint before filling it in.
104	
105	CCR now knows which models are available. What it doesn't know yet is which one to use — that's routing.
106	
107	## Step 3: Configure Routing (Which Model Handles What)
108	
109	Go to the **Routing** page. This is where you decide **which model handles which kind of request.**
110	
111	The one item that matters most is **Default route** — the fallback model used when no special rule matches. Set this and you already have a working minimal config.
112	
113	### Recommended Order
114	
115	1. **Default**: a stable model that can carry the main workload. This is your workhorse.
116	2. **Background**: a cheap, fast model for summaries, context compaction, and other low-priority work.
117	3. **Thinking**: a strong reasoning model for tasks that need depth.
118	4. **Long context**: a large-context model plus the threshold (how many tokens trigger the switch).
119	5. **Image**: a multimodal or Fusion model for image tasks, if you want those routed separately.
120	6. **Web search**: a Fusion model for search-augmented work, if applicable.
121	7. **Fallback**: what happens when the chosen model fails. A common pattern is to retry first, then walk a model chain of backups.
122	
123	> **For your first setup:** Just set Default. You can come back and add Background, Thinking, and the rest whenever you actually need them.
124	
125	### Want Finer Control? (Optional)
126	
127	Click **Add Routing Rule**. What each rule type is for:
128	
129	| Rule type | Use when |
130	| --- | --- |
131	| model-prefix | The client sends a specific model-name prefix and you want to route those requests apart |
132	| subagent | You want to route by subagent signal |
133	| thinking / long-context / image / web-search | You want to route by workload type |
134	| condition | You want to match on request fields, headers, or body content |
135	| rewrite | You need to adjust request-body fields for a compatibility edge case |
136	
137	> **How to tell it worked:** Save, send any request, then open **Logs** and read the `request model`, `resolved provider`, `resolved model`, and status code on that row. If it didn't hit the model you expected, check rule order, match conditions, and fallback first.
138	
139	CCR now knows both *which models exist* and *which one each request should use*. The last step is making your agent actually send its traffic through CCR.
140	
141	## Step 4: Point Your Agent At CCR (Profiles)
142	
143	Go to the **Profiles** page. A Profile lets a chosen agent (Claude Code / Codex / ZCode) use the models and routing settings you configured in CCR, so its requests can be recorded and managed there.
144	
145	Before you start, two options that apply to every profile:
146	
147	- **Scope**:
148	  - **Only opened from CCR**: traffic only goes through CCR when you launch the agent from inside CCR. Your system's default agent setup is untouched. **Strongly recommended for your first try** — easy to experiment, easy to walk back.
149	  - **System default**: the agent uses CCR by default. Switch to this once you're confident the setup is stable.
150	- **Surface**: APP, CLI, or automatic — pick based on how you intend to start the agent.
151	- **Model**: can be a provider model or a Fusion model.
152	
153	> **One habit to keep:** After you Apply, launch the agent from CCR's "open agent" button. That's what lets Bot and app-related features work.
154	
155	### Claude Code
156	
157	1. Select **Claude Code** in Profiles.
158	2. Pick the **Model** for normal requests.
159	3. If you want a cheaper model for lightweight background work, set **Small fast model**.
160	4. Confirm **Settings file** points at your local Claude Code settings path (the default is usually right).
161	5. Add **Env** variables if you need any.
162	6. Click **Apply**.
163	7. Launch Claude Code via **Open Agent** from CCR.
164	
165	> **Verify:** Send one request, then open **Logs**. The Client should read Claude Code, and the provider/model should match your Routing. If so, the whole chain is live.
166	
167	### Codex
168	
169	1. Select **Codex** in Profiles.
170	2. Confirm **Provider ID** and **Provider Name** (defaults are usually fine).
171	3. Pick the **Model** — a provider model or a Fusion model.
172	4. Confirm **Config file** (the default is Codex's config path).
173	5. If you use a specific Codex CLI build, fill in **Codex CLI path** and **Codex home**; otherwise leave them.
174	6. Toggle **CLI middleware** and **Show all sessions** as needed.
175	7. Click **Apply** and open Codex from CCR.
176	
177	Use **Only opened from CCR** while trialing. Switch to **System default** once you're happy.
178	
179	### ZCode
180	
181	ZCode uses the app surface. Focus on **Model**, **Provider ID**, **Provider Name**, and whether you open it from CCR. The Codex-CLI-only fields don't apply here.
182	
183	### Reuse An Agent You're Already Logged Into (Optional)
184	
185	If your machine is already logged into Claude Code, Codex, or ZCode, you can import it from **Providers** as a **Local Agent Provider**. It then shows up in the model picker like any normal provider — handy for reusing an existing local authorization instead of fetching a new key.
186	
187	At this point you have a **complete, working minimal system**: providers connected → routing set → agent wired in. Next, let's open the observability panels and confirm everything really is behaving the way you configured.
188	
189	## Step 5: Open The Observability Panels And Confirm Traffic
190	
191	The goal of this step is to make things *visible* — whether requests are arriving, which model they hit, how much they cost, and whether anything errored.
192	
193	### Turn The Switches On First
194	
195	Overview can't show data until CCR is allowed to record it. Go to **Settings → Observability**:
196	
197	1. Turn on **Request logs** — this feeds Logs and most Overview widgets. **Turn it on.**
198	2. Turn on **Agent analysis** if you want agent-level summaries in Observability.
199	3. **Capture network** (under Server → Proxy) is only for inspecting raw traffic. Turn it on solely while debugging, and turn it off when you're done — it records more complete, and therefore more sensitive, information.
200	
201	### What To Look At: Overview
202	
203	Go to **Overview** and click **Edit widgets** to add components. The most useful ones:
204	
205	| Widget | The question it answers |
206	| --- | --- |
207	| System status | Is the gateway running, is there recent activity |
208	| Requests / Success rate | How much traffic, what's the success rate |
209	| Estimated cost | How much money have I spent |
210	| Token mix | Input / output / cache / reasoning token split |
211	| Model distribution | Which models get used the most |
212	| Provider analysis | Which provider has the most traffic, highest latency, most errors |
213	| Account balance | How much balance and quota is left per provider |
214	
215	You can drag widgets around, resize them, switch display variants, delete, or reset to the default layout. A few ready-made layouts:
216	
217	- **Daily glance:** System status, Requests, Success rate, Usage trend, Provider analysis.
218	- **Cost watch:** Estimated cost, Token mix, Model distribution, Account balance.
219	- **Performance hunt:** Average latency, Errors, Provider analysis, Logs.
220	
221	> **What Overview is for:** It answers "is the overall trend healthy." When a number looks off (a model's cost suddenly spikes, say), jump to Logs to inspect the individual requests. Don't try to diagnose a single failure from Overview alone.
222	
223	### What To Look At: Logs
224	
225	**Logs** is your main tool for debugging individual requests. It requires Request logs to be on.
226	
227	The moves you'll use most:
228	
229	- Filter by **status** to isolate successes or failures.
230	- Filter by **Provider / Model** to focus on one upstream.
231	- Filter by **Credential** to focus on one API key.
232	- Use the search box for request id, model name, request content, or response content.
233	- Click any row to drill into headers, request body, response body, errors, duration, tokens, and cost.
234	
235	That's the full usage loop. You're now a competent CCR user. Everything below is "advanced" — come back to it when you need it.
236	
237	## Advanced: Compose A Model With Tools Using Fusion
238	
239	Go to the **Fusion** page. Its job: **bundle a base model with a tool capability into a new model option** you can then select in Routing or Profiles just like any normal model.
240	
241	Typical use cases: give a model the ability to see images, search the web, or call an MCP tool.
242	
243	### Create A Fusion Model
244	
245	1. Click **Add Fusion**.
246	2. Enter a **New model** alias. Name it after the capability — something with a `vision`, `search`, or `tool` suffix.
247	3. Pick the **Base model** — the one that gives the final answer.
248	4. Pick a built-in tool or a custom MCP tool under **Tools**.
249	5. If you chose the image tool, configure the **Vision model**; if you chose search, configure the **Search provider** and its environment variables.
250	6. Save, then select this Fusion model in Routing or Profiles.
251	
252	> **Play it safe:** Validate a Fusion model in a dedicated profile first. Once you're sure it behaves, promote it into global Default or a special route.
253	
254	### Built-In Vision
255	
256	Select `ccr-fusion-builtins / vision_understand`. Good for screenshot diagnosis, OCR, UI comparison, chart reading, and multi-image analysis.
257	
258	Key points:
259	
260	- The **Vision model** must genuinely support image understanding — it's the one that "reads" the image.
261	- The **Base model** is the one that "answers."
262	- Test with a single screenshot before dropping it into a complex agent workflow.
263	
264	### Built-In Web Search
265	
266	Select `ccr-fusion-builtins / web_search`. Supported providers: Brave, Bing, Google CSE, Serper, SerpAPI, Tavily, and Exa.
267	
268	Key points:
269	
270	- Pick a **Search provider** you've actually enabled.
271	- Fill the API key or environment variables it requires under **Provider configuration**.
272	- Test with a question that needs current information (today's weather somewhere, for instance).
273	- If search fails, check the search API key first, then look at Fusion tool errors in Logs.
274	
275	### Custom MCP Tools
276	
277	Click **Add custom MCP** and choose a transport:
278	
279	- **stdio**: a local command-line tool. Fill Command, Arguments, Working directory, and Environment variables.
280	- **streamable-http / sse**: a remote MCP service. Fill URL and Headers.
281	- **Discover tools**: lists the tools the MCP server exposes.
282	- **Request timeout / Startup timeout**: bump these up if the tool or server is slow.
283	
284	> **Tip:** Only wire stable, predictably-fast MCP tools into Fusion. Validate anything risky in a separate profile first.
285	
286	## Advanced: Relay Agent Messages Into IM With Bots
287	
288	Go to **Bots**, configure one, then attach it in **Profiles**.
289	
290	A Bot forwards an agent's messages into IM, and can hand the task off to your phone after you've been idle for a while. Great for long-running jobs, checking progress remotely, or letting a human take over.
291	
292	### Setup Steps
293	
294	1. Open **Bots** and click **Add Bot**.
295	2. Pick a platform. Supported: Weixin iLink, WeCom, Slack, Discord, Telegram, LINE, Feishu, DingTalk.
296	3. Pick an auth method — the fields differ by platform (Bot Token, OAuth, App Secret, QR Login, and so on).
297	4. Fill in whatever the platform asks for: IDs, tokens, secrets, signing secrets, or robot code.
298	5. Save the bot.
299	6. Open **Profiles** and edit the agent profile you want to attach it to.
300	7. Turn on **Bot** and select the bot you just created.
301	8. Turn on **Forward agent messages** if you want the agent's output relayed too.
302	9. Turn on **Handoff**, set **Idle seconds**, and pick a scanned Wi-Fi or Bluetooth target if you want phone handoff.
303	
304	> **Note:** Bots currently only forward agent messages produced inside an app opened through CCR. Messages from the CLI are not forwarded. Handoff target scanning is available in the Electron desktop app.
305	
306	### Per-Platform Guides
307	
308	Every platform has its own page with the full walk-through (how to create the app on the platform, a field-by-field mapping, and a troubleshooting FAQ):
309	
310	- [Slack](/en/relay-agents-in-im-with-bots/slack)
311	- [Discord](/en/relay-agents-in-im-with-bots/discord)
312	- [Telegram](/en/relay-agents-in-im-with-bots/telegram)
313	- [LINE](/en/relay-agents-in-im-with-bots/line)
314	- [Weixin](/en/relay-agents-in-im-with-bots/weixin-ilink)
315	- [WeCom](/en/relay-agents-in-im-with-bots/wecom)
316	- [Feishu](/en/relay-agents-in-im-with-bots/feishu)
317	- [DingTalk](/en/relay-agents-in-im-with-bots/dingtalk)
318	
319	## When Something Goes Wrong, Look Here
320	
321	CCR gives you three debugging surfaces: **Logs** (request history), **Observability** (agent summaries), and **Networking** (temporary raw captures).
322	
323	### Quick Reference
324	
325	| Symptom | Check first |
326	| --- | --- |
327	| Agent isn't using CCR | Is the Server running, did you Apply the profile, did you open the agent from CCR, is the Scope right |
328	| Request hits the wrong model | Routing Default, rule order, match conditions, fallback — then resolved model in Logs |
329	| Provider auth fails (401/403) | API key, credential, Base URL, protocol, extra headers |
330	| model not found (404) | Is the model in the provider's list, does the model Routing selected actually exist |
331	| Fusion tool never gets called | Fusion tool selection, does the Vision model support images, is the search key right, MCP Discover tools and timeout |
332	| Requests time out | Is the upstream itself slow, is a Fusion tool slow, did you set timeout too low |
333	| Cost suddenly spikes | Filter by model, then look at token mix and request body size |
334	| One key keeps failing | Filter by Credential; if it's really that key, disable it if needed |
335	| Bot receives no messages | Is Bot enabled in the profile, was the app opened from CCR, is Forward agent messages on, is the platform token still valid |
336	| Overview has no data | Are Request logs and Agent analysis on, have any new requests actually come in since |
337	
338	### About Network Capture
339	
340	If you need to inspect the rawest possible exchange (request/response summary, headers, query, body, raw), turn on **Server → Capture network** and open **Networking**. You can pause, resume, refresh, and clear captures.
341	
342	> **Reminder:** Network capture records very complete — and therefore sensitive — information. **Turn it on only while debugging, and off when you're done.** Don't leave it running long-term.
343	
344	## A Few Habits Worth Keeping
345	
346	- **Treat secrets as secrets.** API keys, bot tokens, secrets, and usage endpoints are sensitive. Configure them only in a trusted environment, and never send them to an unverified service.
347	- **Test before you change globals.** Before touching global Routing or Default, validate the change in a separate profile first. Saves you from disrupting an agent that's actively in use.
348	- **Skim Logs now and then.** A periodic glance at errors, tokens, cost, and latency catches problems before they grow.
349	- **Keep backup keys for important providers.** Give critical providers several Credentials with priority, weight, and limits, so one dead key doesn't take everything down.
350	- **Verify deeplinks before importing.** Before importing a provider via a `ccr://provider?...` link, glance at the source, Base URL, protocol, and model list, then confirm.
351	
352	---
353	
354	That's the whole loop: **connect providers → set routing → wire in an agent → turn on observability → extend as needed.** From here it's just usage — come back to whichever section matches the problem in front of you. Enjoy.
355

events.content:
1	---
2	title: Claude Code Router
3	pageTitle: Documentation
4	eyebrow: Product Documentation
5	lead: Claude Code Router (CCR) is a local gateway that sits between coding agents and model providers. It manages credentials, routes requests according to configurable rules, provides observability, and supports multi-model fusion and IM bot relay — all through a single control plane.
6	---
7	
8	## Architecture Overview
9	
10	CCR operates as a local HTTP server (default port `8080`). It receives LLM API requests from coding agents and forwards them to configured model providers. All traffic passes through a single process, making routing, credential management, and observability accessible in one place.
11	
12	```
13	Agent (Claude Code / Codex / ZCode / …)
14	        │
15	        ▼
16	   CCR (localhost:8080)
17	        │
18	        ├──► Provider A (e.g. Anthropic)
19	        ├──► Provider B (e.g. OpenAI)
20	        └──► Provider C (e.g. custom OpenAI-compatible endpoint)
21	```
22	
23	CCR supports the following provider protocols:
24	
25	| Protocol | Use case |
26	|---|---|
27	| Anthropic Messages API | Anthropic models through native protocol |
28	| OpenAI-compatible | OpenAI, third-party providers, and self-hosted models that expose an `/v1/chat/completions` endpoint |
29	
30	Fusion models, described later in this document, allow a base model to be augmented with tools such as web search, vision, or custom MCP tools.
31	
32	## Prerequisites
33	
34	Before installing CCR, verify the following:
35	
36	- **Operating system**: macOS 12+, Windows 10/11, or Linux (x86_64, arm64)
37	- **Network**: Outbound HTTPS access to model provider APIs (Anthropic, OpenAI, or custom endpoints)
38	- **Port**: TCP `8080` available on localhost (configurable)
39	- **Memory**: ~200 MB RAM for the CCR process under typical load
40	- **Node.js** (optional): Required only when running CCR via CLI (`npx`). The desktop application bundles its own runtime.
41	
42	## Installation
43	
44	### Desktop Application
45	
46	Download the installer for your platform from the [releases page](https://github.com/musistudio/claude-code-router/releases).
47	
48	- **macOS**: `.dmg` for Intel, `.dmg` for Apple Silicon
49	- **Windows**: `.exe` installer
50	- **Linux**: `.AppImage`
51	
52	After installation, launch CCR. The **Server** page displays the process status. Enable **Auto start** on that page if the process should launch at login.
53	
54	### Headless / CLI (Alternative)
55	
56	For server or CI/CD environments, CCR can be installed via npm:
57	
58	```bash
59	npm install -g claude-code-router
60	ccr start
61	```
62	
63	When running headless, configuration is managed through the config file (see [Configuration File Format](#configuration-file-format)).
64	
65	### Configuration File Format
66	
67	CCR stores its configuration as a JSON file. The default location is:
68	
69	- **macOS**: `~/Library/Application Support/claude-code-router/config.json`
70	- **Windows**: `%APPDATA%/claude-code-router/config.json`
71	- **Linux**: `~/.config/claude-code-router/config.json`
72	
73	The file is maintained by the desktop UI and can also be edited directly when running headless. Back up this file to version control or a secrets manager for team deployment.
74	
75	## Quick Start
76	
77	A minimal working configuration requires three steps:
78	
79	1. **Add a provider** with valid credentials
80	2. **Set a default route** pointing to that provider
81	3. **Point an agent** at `http://localhost:8080`
82	
83	An agent that sends a request to `http://localhost:8080` will reach the provider selected by the routing rules.
84	
85	## Configuration
86	
87	### Providers
88	
89	A provider is an upstream model service that CCR forwards requests to. CCR needs at least one provider configured.
90	
91	**Adding a provider:**
92	
93	1. Open **Providers** from the sidebar.
94	2. Click **Add Provider**.
95	3. Select a protocol preset from the dropdown. Presets auto-fill the Base URL for common services:
96	   - Anthropic (Messages API)
97	   - OpenAI (`/v1` compatible)
98	4. Enter the provider URL if not using a preset.
99	5. (Optional) Add a custom name.
100	
101	After the provider entry is created, supply credentials.
102	
103	#### Credentials
104	
105	Navigate to the **Credentials** tab for the provider. Enter at least one API key.
106	
107	CCR checks each credential against the provider endpoint and shows a green indicator when the key validates. Validation covers:
108	
109	- Network reachability of the provider URL
110	- API key format and authentication response
111	- Protocol compatibility (HTTP status / JSON structure)
112	
113	Save only after all listed checks show green.
114	
115	**Multiple keys**: Add more than one credential to enable round-robin or fallback across keys. CCR cycles through keys on rate-limit responses and provider errors.
116	
117	**Account page**: The **Account** tab on a provider shows quota and usage when the provider exposes that information through its API. Availability depends on the provider implementation.
118	
119	#### Provider Options Reference
120	
121	| Option | Required | Description |
122	|---|---|---|
123	| Protocol | Yes | Anthropic Messages or OpenAI-compatible |
124	| Base URL | Yes | Provider endpoint URL |
125	| API Key | Yes | Authentication key for the provider |
126	| Custom Name | No | Display name in the UI and routing rules |
127	
128	### Routing
129	
130	Routing rules determine which provider handles each request. CCR evaluates rules in order; the first matching rule wins. If no rule matches, the **Default** route is used.
131	
132	**Default route** — the fallback model used when no other rule applies. Setting a Default is sufficient for a single-provider setup.
133	
134	**Rule types:**
135	
136	| Rule type | Matches on | Example use |
137	|---|---|---|
138	| Model name | Exact or prefix match on the `model` field | Route `claude-sonnet-4-*` to Anthropic, `gpt-*` to OpenAI |
139	| Agent | The agent profile that originated the request | Use a cheaper model for a documentation agent, a larger model for a coding agent |
140	| Tool | Match requests coming from a specific MCP tool | Route search-tool requests to a provider with web-search capability |
141	| Fusion | Route based on whether a Fusion model is in use | Direct vision-augmented requests to a multimodal provider |
142	
143	**Evaluation order**: Rules are evaluated top-to-bottom. Reorder rules by dragging. The Default route is always evaluated last.
144	
145	**Verification**: After saving a routing configuration, send a request from any connected agent. Open **Logs** and confirm the request appears with the expected provider and model.
146	
147	### Profiles
148	
149	A Profile packages an agent's connection settings — endpoint URL, API key, and optional capabilities such as Bot relay.
150	
151	**Supported agents:**
152	
153	- **Claude Code**: Set the environment variable `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL=http://localhost:8080` before launching Claude Code. CCR provides a one-click import that writes this configuration.
154	- **Codex (OpenAI Codex CLI)**: Set `OPENAI_BASE_URL=http://localhost:8080` and `OPENAI_API_KEY=ccr` (or any non-empty value — CCR authenticates by credential, not by agent key).
155	- **ZCode**: Configure the base URL to `http://localhost:8080` in ZCode settings.
156	- **Generic OpenAI-compatible agent**: Any tool that accepts a custom base URL and API key.
157	
158	**Profile options:**
159	
160	| Option | Description |
161	|---|---|
162	| Base URL | Must point to `http://localhost:8080` (or the host/port where CCR is listening) |
163	| API Key | For CCR, any non-empty value. CCR does not authenticate agents by this key — credentials are managed on the provider side |
164	| Bot | Enable to relay agent messages to IM platforms |
165	
166	After applying a Profile, launch the agent through CCR's **Open Agent** button. This ensures Bot and other CCR-managed capabilities are injected correctly.
167	
168	## Observability
169	
170	### Overview
171	
172	The **Overview** page displays aggregate metrics for traffic passing through CCR:
173	
174	| Widget | What it shows |
175	|---|---|
176	| Request volume | Total requests, grouped by time window |
177	| Latency distribution | P50 / P95 / P99 response times per provider |
178	| Token usage | Input and output token counts, per provider and model |
179	| Error rate | Percentage of non-2xx responses, per provider |
180	| Cost estimate | Approximate spend based on provider pricing tables |
181	
182	Overview widgets are intended for monitoring trends and spotting anomalies. For investigating a single failed request, use Logs.
183	
184	### Logs
185	
186	The **Logs** page records every request and response that passes through CCR. Each entry includes:
187	
188	- Timestamp
189	- Agent that sent the request
190	- Provider and model that served it
191	- Request payload (truncatable)
192	- Response payload (truncatable)
193	- HTTP status code
194	- Latency in milliseconds
195	
196	**Filtering**: Filter by time range, agent, provider, model, or status code.
197	
198	**Network capture**: Enable **Capture network** in Logs settings to record raw HTTP exchanges. This is useful for debugging protocol-level issues with custom providers. Disable capture when not actively debugging — captured payloads include API keys in headers.
199	
200	## Fusion Models
201	
202	A Fusion model combines a base model with one or more tool capabilities to create a new model option that appears in the routing model picker.
203	
204	**Available augmentations:**
205	
206	| Augmentation | Description |
207	|---|---|
208	| Vision | Adds image understanding via screen capture or file attachment |
209	| Web search | Adds live web search results to model context |
210	| MCP tool | Attaches a custom MCP server tool to the model |
211	
212	**Creating a Fusion model:**
213	
214	1. Open **Fusion** from the sidebar.
215	2. Select a base model — the provider and model that will handle the core request.
216	3. Add one or more augmentations.
217	4. Name the resulting Fusion model.
218	5. Save.
219	
220	The Fusion model appears as a selectable model in routing rules. When an agent requests that model, CCR invokes the base model with the attached tools, combining outputs into a single response.
221	
222	**Validation**: Test a Fusion model with a dedicated Profile before using it in production routing. For vision Fusion, verify with a screenshot. For web search, verify with a query whose answer depends on current information.
223	
224	## Bots (IM Relay)
225	
226	Bots forward agent messages to instant-messaging platforms and can hand off active tasks to a mobile device when the desktop is idle.
227	
228	### Modes
229	
230	| Mode | Behavior |
231	|---|---|
232	| **Forward** | Every agent message is forwarded to the configured IM channel |
233	| **Handoff** | Messages are forwarded only after the desktop has been idle for the configured duration (see **Idle seconds** in Profile settings) |
234	
235	### Setup
236	
237	1. Open **Bots** from the sidebar.
238	2. Click **Add Bot** and select the target platform.
239	3. Provide the required credentials for that platform.
240	4. Save the Bot.
241	5. Open the target Agent Profile, enable **Bot**, select the Bot, and choose Forward or Handoff.
242	6. Reopen the agent from CCR.
243	
244	### Supported Platforms
245	
246	CCR supports the following IM platforms for Bot relay. Each requires platform-specific setup described in its dedicated page:
247	
248	- [DingTalk](/en/relay-agents-in-im-with-bots/dingtalk)
249	- [Discord](/en/relay-agents-in-im-with-bots/discord)
250	- [Feishu (Lark)](/en/relay-agents-in-im-with-bots/feishu)
251	- [LINE](/en/relay-agents-in-im-with-bots/line)
252	- [Slack](/en/relay-agents-in-im-with-bots/slack)
253	- [Telegram](/en/relay-agents-in-im-with-bots/telegram)
254	- [WeCom (Enterprise WeChat)](/en/relay-agents-in-im-with-bots/wecom)
255	- [Weixin (iLink)](/en/relay-agents-in-im-with-bots/weixin-ilink)
256	
257	Each platform page covers: required credentials, where to obtain them in the platform's developer console, and common troubleshooting steps.
258	
259	## Troubleshooting
260	
261	### Diagnostic Entry Points
262	
263	When a request does not behave as expected, investigate in the following order:
264	
265	1. **Logs** — confirm the request reached CCR, identify the provider and model it was routed to, and check the HTTP status code
266	2. **Overview** — check error rate and latency for the provider to see if the issue is systemic
267	3. **Network capture** — enable for raw request/response inspection when Logs do not contain enough detail
268	
269	### Common Symptoms
270	
271	| Symptom | Likely cause | Check |
272	|---|---|---|
273	| Agent cannot connect to CCR | CCR process not running, port conflict, or firewall | Confirm the Server page shows "Running". Verify port `8080` is not in use by another process. Check firewall rules. |
274	| Request reaches CCR but returns 5xx | Provider credentials invalid or expired | Open the provider's **Credentials** tab and verify the key validates (green indicator). Rotate the key if needed. |
275	| Request returns 4xx | Model name not recognized by provider, or protocol mismatch | Verify the model name in the request matches a model the provider supports. Confirm the provider protocol is correct. |
276	| Request routed to unexpected provider | Routing rule order or Default route misconfigured | Open **Routing** and review rule order. Check that no higher-priority rule matches the request unintentionally. |
277	| High latency | Provider overloaded, network path, or large payload | Check latency distribution in **Overview**. Test the provider directly (outside CCR) to isolate the issue. |
278	| Bot does not receive messages | Bot credentials invalid, platform-side configuration incomplete, or agent not reopened after Bot toggle | Verify Bot fields in **Bots** page. Revisit the platform-specific setup guide. Reopen the agent from CCR. |
279	| Fusion model error | Base model does not support the augmentation, or augmentation tool unavailable | Test the base model directly first. For web search, verify the search service is reachable. For MCP Fusion, confirm the MCP server is running. |
280	| Handoff does not trigger | Idle detection not working, or Handoff selected but Forward intended | Verify **Idle seconds** is set. Confirm Handoff vs Forward setting in the Profile. |
281	| Rate limiting from provider | Too many concurrent requests on a single key | Add additional credentials to the provider for CCR to cycle through. |
282	
283	### Network Capture
284	
285	Network capture records every HTTP request and response at the wire level. Enable it in **Logs settings** → **Capture network**.
286	
287	**Security note**: Captured data includes API keys in request headers. Delete capture files after debugging and do not commit them to version control.
288	
289	## Operational Best Practices
290	
291	- **Rotate API keys regularly.** Use provider dashboards to issue new keys and retire old ones. Update CCR credentials immediately.
292	- **Test routing changes with a dedicated Profile** before modifying the Default route or rules used by production agents.
293	- **Review Logs periodically** even when nothing is broken — it surfaces slow providers, unexpected model usage, and credential issues before they escalate.
294	- **Maintain spare credentials.** Having a second key for each provider prevents a single key rotation from blocking all traffic.
295	- **Back up the config file.** The config JSON contains all provider entries, routing rules, and Bot configurations. Store it securely.
296	- **Audit Fusion and Bot configurations** after CCR upgrades. New versions may introduce additional capabilities or change default behavior.
297	
298	## Reference
299	
300	### Configuration File Format
301	
302	CCR stores its configuration as a JSON file at the OS-specific path listed in [Configuration File Format](#configuration-file-format). The file is structured as follows:
303	
304	```json
305	{
306	  "version": 3,
307	  "port": 8080,
308	  "providers": [
309	    {
310	      "id": "uuid",
311	      "name": "Anthropic",
312	      "protocol": "anthropic",
313	      "baseUrl": "https://api.anthropic.com",
314	      "credentials": [
315	        { "key": "sk-ant-...", "label": "primary" }
316	      ]
317	    }
318	  ],
319	  "routing": {
320	    "default": "claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
321	    "rules": []
322	  },
323	  "profiles": [],
324	  "bots": [],
325	  "fusionModels": []
326	}
327	```
328	
329	When editing the file directly, restart CCR for changes to take effect. The desktop UI writes changes back to this file on every **Save** or **Apply** action.
330	
331	### Environment Variables
332	
333	| Variable | Purpose |
334	|---|---|
335	| `CCR_PORT` | Override the default listen port (default: `8080`) |
336	| `CCR_CONFIG_PATH` | Override the config file location |
337	| `CCR_LOG_LEVEL` | Set log verbosity: `debug`, `info`, `warn`, `error` (default: `info`) |
338	
339	### Supported Model Providers
340	
341	CCR is compatible with any provider that exposes an Anthropic Messages API or OpenAI-compatible `/v1/chat/completions` endpoint. This includes, but is not limited to:
342	
343	- Anthropic
344	- OpenAI
345	- Azure OpenAI
346	- Google Vertex AI (via OpenAI-compatible adapter)
347	- AWS Bedrock (via OpenAI-compatible adapter)
348	- OpenRouter
349	- Self-hosted models (vLLM, Ollama, llama.cpp server)
350	
351	### Version
352	
353	This documentation applies to CCR v3.0.0 and later. Refer to the [GitHub releases page](https://github.com/musistudio/claude-code-router/releases) for changelogs and upgrade notes.
354

fileContexts.content:
1	---
2	title: Claude Code Router 使用指南
3	pageTitle: 使用指南
4	eyebrow: 上手指南
5	lead: 这是一份手把手带你跑通 Claude Code Router 的指南。我们会从下载安装开始，一步步接入你的第一个模型、把 Agent 接进来、看到请求真的走了 CCR。不需要你手动编辑任何配置文件。
6	---
7	
8	如果你是第一次用 CCR，建议从头顺着读一遍——大概十几分钟，你就能把整套链路跑通。已经有经验的读者可以直接跳到对应章节，每一步都包含了「怎么确认自己没做错」的验证方法。
9	
10	## CCR 能帮你做什么
11	
12	一句话：**它让你在一个地方管理 Claude Code、Codex、ZCode 等 Agent 要用的模型和 Key，并按任务选择合适的模型。**
13	
14	这样做的好处是：
15	
16	- 你不用在每个 Agent 里重复配置模型和 Key。
17	- 不同任务可以用不同模型：简单任务用便宜的快模型，难题用强模型，看图用多模态模型，需要联网时用带搜索的模型。
18	- 一个模型出错时，可以自动换到备用模型。
19	- 你可以在 Logs 里看到每次请求用了哪个模型、是否成功、费用大概花在哪。
20	
21	后面的步骤会带你完成三件事：接入模型、设置路由、让 Agent 通过 CCR 使用这些配置。正常使用时，你只需要在界面里按步骤配置，不需要手动编辑配置文件。
22	
23	## 第一步：安装并启动 CCR
24	
25	### 下载安装
26	
27	1. 打开 [GitHub Releases](https://github.com/musistudio/claude-code-router/releases) 页面。
28	2. 按你的系统下载对应的安装包：
29	   - macOS：`.dmg` 或 `.zip`
30	   - Windows：`.exe`
31	   - Linux：`.AppImage`
32	3. 像装普通软件一样安装并打开 **Claude Code Router**。
33	
34	### 启动 CCR 服务
35	
36	打开 App 后，进入 **Server** 页面，点击 **Start**。
37	
38	> **怎么算成功：** Server 页面显示正在运行。如果你想以后开 App 就自动启动，把 **Auto start** 打开。
39	
40	到这里 CCR 本身就跑起来了，但还不能处理请求，因为你还没有接入模型。下一步我们就来完成这件事。
41	
42	## 第二步：接入你的第一个 Provider
43	
44	Provider 就是 CCR 转发请求要去到的「上游模型服务」，比如 OpenRouter、DeepSeek、Z.AI，或者任何兼容 OpenAI / Anthropic / Gemini 协议的服务。
45	
46	### 添加 Provider
47	
48	1. 进入 **Providers** 页面，点击 **Add Provider**。
49	2. 先在 **Provider preset** 里挑一个内置预设。预设的好处是它会自动帮你填好常见的 Base URL、图标和协议，省得你查文档。如果你的服务不在列表里，选 **Other / custom API endpoint**。
50	3. 依次填写：
51	   - **Name**：在 CCR 里显示的名字，起个短一点、认得出的就行，比如 `openrouter`、`deepseek`。
52	   - **Base URL**：上游服务地址。自定义 Provider 一定要确认地址里包含了正确的 API 路径。
53	   - **Protocol**：上游真正支持的协议。选错了通常连通性检查就过不了。拿不准的话用下面的协议探测。
54	   - **API Key**：填你的 Key。只用一个 Key 的话填这里就够了。
55	   - **Models**：把这个 Provider 要暴露给 CCR 的模型列出来，模型选择器就是从这里取选项的。
56	4. 填完别急着保存，先做连通性检查（见下）。
57	
58	### 协议怎么选
59	
60	| 协议 | 适用场景 |
61	| --- | --- |
62	| OpenAI Chat Completions | 绝大多数 OpenAI 兼容服务（最常见） |
63	| OpenAI Responses | 支持 Responses API 的服务 |
64	| Anthropic Messages | Anthropic 官方或兼容它的服务 |
65	| Gemini Generate Content | Gemini 官方或兼容它的服务 |
66	
67	> **拿不准协议时：** 先用 App 自带的协议探测（Protocol probe）扫一下。探测结果只是参考，最终还是要以 Provider 官方文档和连通性检查为准。
68	
69	### 保存前做这三项检查
70	
71	把问题在这一步卡住，后面 Routing 和 Agent 出错时就不会乱猜了：
72	
73	1. **Protocol probe**：确认这个 Base URL 到底支持哪些协议。
74	2. **Model connectivity check**：挑一两个模型实际发个测试请求，看能不能通。
75	3. **Account usage test**（可选）：如果你开了用量统计，顺便确认余额和配额能正常读出来。
76	
77	三项都绿了，再点保存。
78	
79	> **怎么算成功：** 保存后这个 Provider 出现在列表里，至少一个模型的状态是可用的。你可以随手发一个测试请求，应该能拿到正常响应。
80	
81	### 想用多个 Key？（可选）
82	
83	个人用单 Key 够了；如果是团队或高频调用，建议打开 **Credentials** 加多条 Key，CCR 会自动轮换：
84	
85	1. 在 Provider 表单里打开 **Credentials**。
86	2. 点 **Add credential**。
87	3. 给每条 Key 填一个 **Label**，方便在 Logs 里认出来。
88	4. 设 **Priority**：数字越小越优先用。
89	5. 设 **Weight**：同一优先级里，权重越大分到的请求越多。
90	6. 如果 Key 有配额限制，填 **Limits**，方便看出哪条快到顶了。
91	7. 保存后发几条测试请求，到 Logs 里按 Credential 筛一下，确认轮换符合预期。
92	
93	### 想在面板上看余额？（可选）
94	
95	如果你希望 Overview 上直接显示某个 Provider 的余额、剩余配额，就打开 **Account / Usage**：
96	
97	1. 选一个 usage 接入方式。有内置标准接口就优先用内置；覆盖不到再考虑 HTTP JSON 或 Plugin。
98	2. 填好认证方式和 endpoint。
99	3. 点 **Test** 读一次数据。
100	4. 从返回结果里勾选「余额、剩余量、已用量、重置时间」等字段。
101	5. 回到 Overview，加一个 **Account balance** 组件。
102	
103	> **安全提醒：** 别把 Provider 的 API Key 发给来路不明的 usage endpoint。自定义 endpoint 一定要先确认域名和权限范围再填。
104	
105	接入 Provider 之后，CCR 已经知道「有哪些模型可以用」了，但它还不知道「该用哪一个」。下一步就来定路由。
106	
107	## 第三步：设置路由（决定请求去哪个模型）
108	
109	进入 **Routing** 页面。这里控制的是：**不同的请求，分别交给哪个模型处理。**
110	
111	最核心的一项是 **Default route**——它是所有没有命中任何特殊规则时的兜底模型。先把这一项配好，你就已经有一个能用的最小配置了。
112	
113	### 推荐的配置顺序
114	
115	1. **Default**：选一个稳定、能扛主要任务的模型。这是你的「主力」。
116	2. **Background**：选一个便宜、快的模型，用来跑后台总结、上下文压缩这类不重要的活。
117	3. **Thinking**：选一个推理能力强的模型，留给需要深度思考的任务。
118	4. **Long context**：选一个上下文窗口大的模型，并设好触发阈值（超过多少 token 才切到它）。
119	5. **Image**：如果图片任务你想走 Fusion 或某个多模态模型，在这里指定。
120	6. **Web search**：如果搜索任务走 Fusion，在这里指定。
121	7. **Fallback**：选一个模型挂掉时的兜底策略。常见做法是先 retry，失败再按一条 model chain 依次尝试备用模型。
122	
123	> **新手建议：** 第一次配置时，把 Default 配好就够了。Background、Thinking 这些都可以等你有需要了再回来加。
124	
125	### 需要更精细的控制？（可选）
126	
127	点 **Add Routing Rule** 可以加规则。每种规则的用途：
128	
129	| 规则类型 | 什么时候用 |
130	| --- | --- |
131	| model-prefix | 客户端传了特定模型名前缀时，分流到指定模型 |
132	| subagent | 按 subagent 信号选模型 |
133	| thinking / long-context / image / web-search | 按任务类型分流 |
134	| condition | 按请求字段、Header 或请求体内容匹配 |
135	| rewrite | 命中规则后改写请求体的某些字段，处理少数兼容性问题 |
136	
137	> **怎么算成功：** 保存后随便发一个请求，打开 **Logs**，看这条记录里的 `request model`、`resolved provider`、`resolved model` 和状态码。如果命中的不是你想要的模型，优先检查：规则的顺序、匹配条件、以及 fallback 设置。
138	
139	到这一步，CCR 已经知道「有哪些模型」和「请求该去哪个模型」了。最后一步，是让你的 Agent 真的把请求发给 CCR。
140	
141	## 第四步：让 Agent 走 CCR（Profile）
142	
143	进入 **Profiles** 页面。Profile 的作用，是让你选定的 Agent（Claude Code / Codex / ZCode）使用 CCR 里的模型和路由配置，这样请求才会经过 CCR 记录和管理。
144	
145	开始前，先理解两个通用选项：
146	
147	- **Scope**：
148	  - 选 **Only opened from CCR**：只在你从 CCR 里点开这个 Agent 时才走 CCR，不影响你系统里原本的 Agent 设置。**强烈建议先用这个**，方便试用又不出岔子。
149	  - 选 **System default**：让这个 Agent 默认就走 CCR。等你确认稳定了再切到这个。
150	- **Surface**：选 APP、CLI 或自动，取决于你打算从哪里启动这个 Agent。
151	- **Model**：可以选某个 Provider 模型，也可以选 Fusion 模型。
152	
153	> **一条通用习惯：** Apply 之后，尽量从 CCR 里的「打开 Agent」按钮来启动它，这样 Bot、App 相关的能力才能生效。
154	
155	### Claude Code
156	
157	1. 在 Profiles 里选 **Claude Code**。
158	2. 选 **Model**（常规请求的模型）。
159	3. 如果想让后台轻量任务用便宜模型，设一下 **Small fast model**。
160	4. 确认 **Settings file** 指向你本机 Claude Code 的配置路径（默认值通常就对）。
161	5. 需要额外环境变量就在 **Env** 里加。
162	6. 点 **Apply**。
163	7. 用 **Open Agent** 从 CCR 启动 Claude Code。
164	
165	> **验证：** 发一次请求，然后打开 **Logs**。这条记录的 Client 应该显示为 Claude Code，Provider 和模型应该和你在 Routing 里配的一致。如果是，说明整条链路通了。
166	
167	### Codex
168	
169	1. 在 Profiles 里选 **Codex**。
170	2. 确认 **Provider ID** 和 **Provider Name**（默认值一般直接能用）。
171	3. 选 **Model**，可以是普通 Provider 模型，也可以是 Fusion 模型。
172	4. 确认 **Config file**（默认是 Codex 的配置文件路径）。
173	5. 如果你用的是特定版本的 Codex CLI，填 **Codex CLI path** 和 **Codex home**；用默认安装的话不用填。
174	6. 按需打开 **CLI middleware** 和 **Show all sessions**。
175	7. 点 **Apply**，从 CCR 打开 Codex。
176	
177	试用阶段用 **Only opened from CCR**，确认稳定后再改成 **System default**。
178	
179	### ZCode
180	
181	ZCode 走的是 App surface。配置时关注 **Model**、**Provider ID**、**Provider Name**，以及是否从 CCR 打开。它不需要 Codex CLI 那些字段。
182	
183	### 复用本机已登录的 Agent（可选）
184	
185	如果你这台机器已经登录过 Claude Code、Codex 或 ZCode，可以在 **Providers** 里把它们导入成 **Local Agent Provider**。导入后它们就像普通 Provider 一样出现在模型选择器里，适合复用已有的本地授权，不用再去申请 Key。
186	
187	到这里，你已经拥有了一套**完整可用的最小系统**：Provider 接好了 → 路由配好了 → Agent 接进来了。下一步我们打开观察面板，确认一切真的在按你的预期运转。
188	
189	## 第五步：打开观察面板，确认请求走了 CCR
190	
191	这一步的目的是让你「看得见」——看得见请求来了没有、走了哪个模型、花了多少钱、有没有报错。
192	
193	### 先把开关打开
194	
195	Overview 想有数据，得先让 CCR 开始记录。去 **Settings → Observability**：
196	
197	1. 打开 **Request logs**（这是 Logs 和大部分 Overview 组件的数据来源，**必开**）。
198	2. 打开 **Agent analysis**（如果你想在 Observability 里看 Agent 维度的汇总）。
199	3. **Capture network**（在 Server → Proxy 里）只在需要看原始网络包时才开，排查完记得关掉，因为它记录的信息更完整、也更敏感。
200	
201	### 看什么：Overview
202	
203	进入 **Overview**，点 **Edit widgets** 可以加组件。几个最常用的：
204	
205	| 组件 | 回答的问题 |
206	| --- | --- |
207	| System status | 网关在不在跑、最近有没有活动 |
208	| Requests / Success rate | 请求量多少、成功率多少 |
209	| Estimated cost | 钱花多少了 |
210	| Token mix | 输入/输出/缓存/推理 token 各占多少 |
211	| Model distribution | 哪些模型被用得最多 |
212	| Provider analysis | 哪个 Provider 请求多、延迟高、报错多 |
213	| Account balance | 各 Provider 余额、配额还剩多少 |
214	
215	你可以拖动调整位置和大小、切换展示样式、删除或重置成默认布局。几个现成的布局思路：
216	
217	- **日常盯盘**：System status、Requests、Success rate、Usage trend、Provider analysis。
218	- **盯成本**：Estimated cost、Token mix、Model distribution、Account balance。
219	- **查性能**：Average latency、Errors、Provider analysis、Logs。
220	
221	> **Overview 的定位：** 它回答的是「整体趋势正不正常」。一旦你看到某个数字不对劲（比如某模型成本突然飙高），再跳到 Logs 看具体某条请求。不要只用 Overview 去判断单次失败的原因。
222	
223	### 看什么：Logs
224	
225	**Logs** 是你排查单条请求的主战场。前提是 Request logs 已打开。
226	
227	常用的玩法：
228	
229	- 按**状态**筛成功或失败的请求。
230	- 按 **Provider / Model** 筛某个上游服务。
231	- 按 **Credential** 筛某条 API Key。
232	- 用搜索框找 request id、模型名、请求内容或响应内容。
233	- 点开任意一行，看 Header、请求体、响应体、报错、耗时、token、成本。
234	
235	到这里，你就已经是一名合格的 CCR 使用者了。后面的内容是「进阶」——需要的时候再回来看。
236	
237	## 进阶一：用 Fusion 把模型和工具组合起来
238	
239	进入 **Fusion** 页面。它的作用是：**把「一个基础模型 + 一种工具能力」打包成一个新的模型选项**，保存后你能在 Routing 或 Profiles 里像选普通模型一样选它。
240	
241	典型场景：让某个模型能看图、能联网搜索，或者能调用某个 MCP 工具。
242	
243	### 创建一个 Fusion 模型
244	
245	1. 点 **Add Fusion**。
246	2. 在 **New model** 里填一个别名。建议起个能体现能力的名字，比如带 `vision`、`search`、`tool` 后缀。
247	3. 在 **Base model** 里选负责最终回答的模型。
248	4. 在 **Tools** 里选内置工具或自定义 MCP 工具。
249	5. 选了图像工具，就接着配 **Vision model**；选了搜索工具，就接着配 **Search provider** 和相关环境变量。
250	6. 保存后，去 Routing 或 Profiles 里选这个 Fusion 模型。
251	
252	> **稳妥做法：** 先用一个独立的 Profile 验证 Fusion 模型好不好用，确认没问题了，再把它放到全局 Default 或特殊路由里。
253	
254	### 内置图像能力
255	
256	选 `ccr-fusion-builtins / vision_understand`。适合截图诊断、OCR、UI 对比、图表解读、多图分析。
257	
258	要点：
259	
260	- **Vision model** 必须是真正支持图像理解的模型——它负责「看懂图」。
261	- **Base model** 负责「给出最终答案」。
262	- 先拿一张截图测通了，再把它塞进复杂的 Agent 工作流。
263	
264	### 内置联网搜索
265	
266	选 `ccr-fusion-builtins / web_search`。支持的搜索服务有：Brave、Bing、Google CSE、Serper、SerpAPI、Tavily、Exa。
267	
268	要点：
269	
270	- **Search provider** 选一个你已经开通的服务。
271	- 在 **Provider configuration** 里填该服务要求的 API Key 或环境变量。
272	- 保存后，用一个「需要实时信息」的问题测一下（比如「今天某地天气」）。
273	- 搜索失败的话，先检查搜索服务的 Key，再看 Logs 里的 Fusion 工具报错。
274	
275	### 接自定义 MCP 工具
276	
277	点 **Add custom MCP**，按工具类型选 transport：
278	
279	- **stdio**：本地命令行工具。填 Command、Arguments、Working directory、Environment variables。
280	- **streamable-http / sse**：远程 MCP 服务。填 URL 和 Headers。
281	- **Discover tools**：读出这个 MCP server 暴露了哪些工具。
282	- **Request timeout / Startup timeout**：工具慢或启动慢就适当调大。
283	
284	> **建议：** 只把稳定、响应快的 MCP 工具接进 Fusion。高风险的工具先在独立 Profile 里验证再放开。
285	
286	## 进阶二：把 Agent 消息转发到 IM（Bot）
287	
288	进入 **Bots** 页面，配置好后再到 **Profiles** 里绑定。
289	
290	Bot 能把 Agent 的消息转发到 IM，还能在你空闲一段时间后，把任务接力到手机上继续看。适合长时间运行的任务、远程查看进度、或者人工接管。
291	
292	### 配置步骤
293	
294	1. 打开 **Bots**，点 **Add Bot**。
295	2. 选平台。支持：Weixin iLink、WeCom、Slack、Discord、Telegram、LINE、Feishu、DingTalk。
296	3. 选认证方式，不同平台要填的字段不一样（Bot Token、OAuth、App Secret、QR Login 之类）。
297	4. 填好平台要求的 ID、Token、Secret、Signing Secret 或 Robot Code。
298	5. 保存这个 Bot。
299	6. 打开 **Profiles**，编辑你想接 Bot 的那个 Agent Profile。
300	7. 打开 **Bot** 开关，选刚才创建的 Bot。
301	8. 想把 Agent 的输出也转过去，就开 **Forward agent messages**。
302	9. 想要手机接力，就开 **Handoff**，设好 **Idle seconds**，再选扫描到的 Wi-Fi 或蓝牙目标。
303	
304	> **注意：** Bot 目前只转发「从 CCR 打开的 App」里产生的 Agent 消息，CLI 里跑的消息不会被转发。Handoff 目标扫描需要在 Electron 桌面 App 里使用。
305	
306	### 各平台详细教程
307	
308	每个平台的完整步骤（平台后台怎么建应用、字段对照、排查 FAQ）都有单独一篇：
309	
310	- [Slack](/bot-与-im-接力-agent/slack)
311	- [Discord](/bot-与-im-接力-agent/discord)
312	- [Telegram](/bot-与-im-接力-agent/telegram)
313	- [LINE](/bot-与-im-接力-agent/line)
314	- [微信](/bot-与-im-接力-agent/weixin-ilink)
315	- [企业微信](/bot-与-im-接力-agent/wecom)
316	- [飞书](/bot-与-im-接力-agent/feishu)
317	- [钉钉](/bot-与-im-接力-agent/dingtalk)
318	
319	## 遇到问题时，照着这个查
320	
321	CCR 给了你三个排查入口：**Logs**（看请求历史）、**Observability**（看 Agent 汇总）、**Networking**（看临时网络抓包）。
322	
323	### 快速对照表
324	
325	| 你遇到的现象 | 先查这些 |
326	| --- | --- |
327	| Agent 没走 CCR | Server 在不在跑、Profile 有没有 Apply、Agent 是不是从 CCR 打开的、Scope 对不对 |
328	| 请求命中了错误的模型 | Routing 的 Default、规则顺序、匹配条件、fallback，再看 Logs 里的 resolved model |
329	| Provider 鉴权失败（401/403） | API Key、Credential、Base URL、协议、额外的 Header |
330	| 报 model not found（404） | Provider 的模型列表对不对、Routing 选的那个模型存不存在 |
331	| Fusion 没调用工具 | Fusion 工具选对没、Vision model 支不支持、搜索服务 Key 对不对、MCP 的 Discover tools 和 timeout |
332	| 请求超时 | 上游服务本身慢不慢、Fusion 工具慢不慢、timeout 设小了没 |
333	| 成本突然变高 | 按模型筛一下，看 token 组成和请求体大小 |
334	| 某条 Key 一直失败 | 按 Credential 筛，确认是它的问题后，必要时停用这条 Key |
335	| Bot 收不到消息 | Profile 里 Bot 开关开了没、是不是从 CCR 打开的 App、Forward agent messages 开了没、平台 Token 还有效吗 |
336	| Overview 没有数据 | Request logs 和 Agent analysis 开了没、之后有没有真的产生新请求 |
337	
338	### 关于 Network capture
339	
340	如果你需要看最原始的网络交换（请求/响应的 summary、header、query、body、raw），就去 **Server → Capture network** 打开，再到 **Networking** 看。可以暂停、恢复、刷新、清空。
341	
342	> **提醒：** Network capture 记录的信息很完整，也因此更敏感。**只在排查时打开，查完就关掉**，别长期开着。
343	
344	## 几个值得养成的好习惯
345	
346	- **敏感信息要当心**：API Key、Bot Token、Secret、Usage endpoint 都属于敏感信息，只在可信环境里配置，别发到来路不明的服务。
347	- **改全局路由前先试**：要动全局 Routing 或 Default 时，先用一个单独的 Profile 验证，确认没问题再放开，避免影响正在用的 Agent。
348	- **定期看 Logs**：偶尔翻一下错误、token、成本和延迟，能在问题变大之前发现它。
349	- **重要 Provider 多备 Key**：给重要的 Provider 配多条 Credential，并设好优先级、权重和限制，单 Key 挂了不至于全线中断。
350	- **导入 deeplink 前核对**：通过 `ccr://provider?...` 导入 Provider 配置前，先看一眼来源、Base URL、协议和模型对不对，再点确认。
351	
352	---
353	
354	走到这里，你已经掌握了 CCR 的完整使用闭环：**接入 Provider → 配置路由 → 接上 Agent → 打开观察 → 按需扩展**。剩下的就是用起来，遇到具体问题再回到对应章节查。祝用得顺手。
355

1	---
2	title: DingTalk Bot Setup
3	pageTitle: DingTalk Bot
4	eyebrow: Relay Agents In IM With Bots
5	lead: Route agent messages into DingTalk's enterprise collaboration environment, with relay after your screen locks. This page walks you from creating an app in the DingTalk developer backend to a working setup in CCR.
6	---
7	
8	## Who This Is For
9	
10	DingTalk is for bringing agent messages into an enterprise collaboration environment. CCR connects to DingTalk apps using App Secret auth.
11	
12	> New to bots? Start with the "Relay Agent Messages Into IM With Bots" section of the main guide to understand the overall flow and the Forward/Handoff distinction, then come back here.
13	
14	## The Fields You'll Use
15	
16	| Name in the DingTalk dashboard | CCR field | Required | Notes |
17	| --- | --- | --- | --- |
18	| Client ID / AppKey | App Key | Required | App identifier |
19	| Client Secret / AppSecret | App Secret | Required | App secret |
20	| RobotCode | Robot Code | Optional | May be needed for multi-bot or media scenarios |
21	
22	> Newer DingTalk configures the bot as an "app capability" — don't start from the old standalone "bot" entry.
23	
24	## Step 1: Create A DingTalk App
25	
26	1. Open the [DingTalk developer backend](https://open-dev.dingtalk.com/).
27	2. Log in with your DingTalk account.
28	3. Pick the dev organization to connect.
29	4. Open `应用开发` (App Development) at the top.
30	5. Click `创建应用` (Create App).
31	6. Name it, e.g. `CCR`.
32	7. Fill in the description; leave other options default.
33	8. Click create.
34	
35	## Step 2: Copy The App Key And App Secret
36	
37	1. Open the app you just created.
